serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of iced tamarind drink is approximately 250 g, as typically served at Singaporean hawker centres and drink stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Iced Tamarind — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Iced Tamarind (cr_id G00011034). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; tamarind-based sweetened beverage estimated from fruit juice / sugar-sweetened beverage analogues (GI ~65)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for iced sweetened tamarind drink exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened fruit beverages and tamarind concentrate entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 62.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 101.6 for a 250 g serving (carbohydrate per serving = 62.5 g/100g × 250 g / 100 = 156.25 g; GL = 65 × 156.25 / 100 = 101.6). This is an extremely high glycemic load per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (38.8 g/100g) in a sweetened beverage; exceeds the 5 g/100g SSB sugar gate for hypertension
hypertension.guidance: Persons with hypertension should avoid this sweetened tamarind drink due to its very high sugar content (38.8 g/100g); despite beneficial potassium (628 mg/100g) and low sodium (28 mg/100g), the extreme sugar load promotes insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk — request an unsweetened tamarind water preparation instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 38.8 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g diabetes hard gate; carbohydrate at 62.5 g/100g produces an extremely high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes must strictly avoid this iced tamarind drink; the sugar content of 38.8 g/100g and total carbohydrate of 62.5 g/100g produce a glycemic load of approximately 101.6 per 250 g serving, which will cause acute postprandial hyperglycaemia — an unsweetened tamarind preparation with no added sugar is the only acceptable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high free sugar content (38.8 g/100g) promotes dyslipidaemia, hepatic fat accumulation, and elevated triglycerides, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Persons with cardiovascular disease should avoid this sugar-sweetened tamarind drink; the 38.8 g/100g sugar content substantially exceeds WHO free-sugar guidelines (<10% of energy) and is associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL-cholesterol — the high potassium (628 mg/100g) and iron (2.8 mg/100g) content cannot offset the cardiovascular harm of this sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 38.8 g/100g far exceeds the 15 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold for rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Persons with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this drink; the extreme added sugar content (38.8 g/100g) promotes systemic inflammatory mediators and negates the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefits of tamarind — an unsweetened tamarind preparation would be preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Tamarind Drink (冰罗望子饮料 / Air Asam Jawa) is a popular chilled sweetened beverage served at Singaporean hawker centres, drink stalls, and food courts. It is prepared by dissolving tamarind pulp (Tamarindus indica) in water, sweetening generously with sugar, and serving over ice. The resulting drink has a distinctive sweet-sour flavour profile with a deep amber-brown colour and a slightly viscous, pulpy texture. Tamarind is a leguminous tree fruit native to tropical Africa and widely cultivated across South and Southeast Asia; it is a staple souring and flavouring agent in Singaporean, Malaysian, Indian, and Thai cuisines. Per 100 g, this drink provides 239 kcal, 62.5 g carbohydrate, 38.8 g sugar, 2.8 g protein, 0.6 g fat, and 5.1 g dietary fibre, along with notable potassium (628 mg), magnesium (92 mg), calcium (74 mg), and iron (2.8 mg). Despite the nutritional richness of tamarind itself — which contains polyphenols, tartaric acid, and antioxidants — the high added sugar content in the commercial preparation makes this drink clinically unsuitable for persons with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) but the glycemic load per 250 g serving is extremely high (~101.6), making it one of the most glycaemically impactful beverages in the Singaporean hawker context. Unsweetened tamarind water would retain the beneficial micronutrients and antioxidants without the sugar burden.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in iced tamarind?
Is tamarind drink good for blood pressure?
Is tamarind drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00011034, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI sub-field only) but all other data are exact-match from an approved primary source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this iced tamarind drink preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00011034) or in USDA FoodData Central for a directly equivalent sweetened iced tamarind beverage as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.1 g/100g based on the very low total fat (0.6 g/100g) and the predominantly plant-based composition of tamarind pulp; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for tamarind (raw) which reports negligible saturated fat. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): tamarind is a plant-based food with no cholesterol; this is confirmed by HPB source and consistent with USDA FoodData Central. 4. Glycemic index of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using sugar-sweetened fruit beverage analogues; no direct GI measurement for iced sweetened tamarind drink was found. 5. Glycemic load per serving (250 g) = (65 × 156.25) / 100 = 101.6 — extremely high. 6. food_group updated from 'Mixed' to 'Beverages' and food_subgroup updated to 'Sugar-sweetened beverages' to reflect the actual nature of this product. 7. FoodEx2 code A00KR used as closest defensible anchor for fruit-based soft drink; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for iced sweetened tamarind drink.
